ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for beef brisket rice noodle soup; mapped to the closest parent class 'noodle dish' (FOODON_03315595). A more specific term does not exist in the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_description: Noodle soup dish — beef-based broth with rice noodles; closest FoodEx2 category for composite noodle soup dishes.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for beef brisket rice noodle. A0CKP (pasta and noodle dishes) used as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical food concept found for this specific dish. SNOMED CT coverage of composite Asian noodle dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of beef brisket rice noodle as typically served in a Singapore hawker centre or restaurant is approximately 400 g, comprising rice noodles, braised beef brisket pieces, and clear broth.

nutrient_source.title: Beef Brisket Rice Noodle 清汤牛腩小锅米线 — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Beef Brisket Rice Noodle (清汤牛腩小锅米线), cr_id G00010493, with confidence 0.95. All per-100g macro and micronutrient values are taken verbatim from the HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — rice noodles (analogous estimate)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. GI estimated at 61 based on rice noodles as the dominant carbohydrate source (GI ~61 per University of Sydney GI Database for boiled rice noodles). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (61 × 25.19 g/100g × 400 g serving / 100) / 100 = (61 × 100.76 g) / 100 ≈ 61.5 g. The beef brisket and broth components contribute negligible carbohydrate and are not expected to materially alter the GI of the dish.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100 g; a 400 g bowl delivers approximately 924 mg sodium, representing ~40% of the 2300 mg daily limit, partially offset by potassium at 327 mg/100 g (1308 mg per bowl).
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced-salt broth and consume only a portion of the soup liquid to limit sodium intake, while the favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio (0.71) provides partial DASH-diet alignment.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 25.19 g/100 g with a medium GI (~61) from rice noodles; dietary fibre at 3.7 g/100 g moderates postprandial glucose response, and sugar is negligible at 0.21 g/100 g.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size to 200–250 g of noodles and broth, prioritise the protein and vegetable components, and monitor postprandial blood glucose given the moderate glycaemic index of rice noodles.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beef brisket is a fatty red meat; total fat is 4.21 g/100 g with saturated fat data unavailable from the HPB source, and sodium at 231 mg/100 g poses a cumulative cardiovascular risk in a full-bowl serving.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption frequency to occasional servings, request lean brisket or reduced portions of meat, minimise broth intake to reduce sodium load, and favour dishes with fish or legume protein as a regular alternative.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef brisket is a fatty red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies inflammatory pathways via TNF-alpha and IL-6.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid regular consumption of beef brisket noodle dishes and substitute with chicken, fish, or tofu-based noodle alternatives to reduce pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Brisket Rice Noodle (清汤牛腩小锅米线) is a popular Chinese-style noodle soup dish widely served in Singapore hawker centres and casual restaurants. The dish consists of tender braised beef brisket slices served over smooth, silky rice noodles (米线, mǐ xiàn) in a clear, lightly seasoned beef broth (清汤, qīng tāng). The brisket is slow-braised until fork-tender, yielding a rich, savoury flavour with a slightly gelatinous texture from the collagen-rich cut. The broth is typically clear and mild compared to spiced or red-braised variants, making it a lighter option within the beef noodle category. Garnishes commonly include spring onion, fried shallots, and occasionally blanched vegetables. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, 25.19 g carbohydrate, 3.7 g dietary fibre, and 231 mg sodium. The dish is a moderate-GI food (~61) due to the rice noodle base, and the fibre content helps moderate postprandial glucose response. Potassium at 327 mg/100 g and iron at 2.55 mg/100 g are notable micronutrients. Clinically, the dish is conditionally suitable for individuals managing hypertension and type 2 diabetes with portion control, but is not recommended for those with rheumatoid arthritis due to the pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid content of beef brisket. The dish is gluten-free and dairy-free, making it accessible to individuals with those dietary restrictions.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010493) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. The HPB is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. The match is exact — the HPB entry is specifically for this dish by name. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (boiled rice noodles) from the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source, and the estimation basis is documented.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for G00010493 as of 2026-04-16. 2. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB source for this dish. 3. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 in the HPB source; this is accepted as a confirmed reported value from the approved source (HPB G00010493), not a zero-macro justification issue as cholesterol is not a core macro. 4. GI of 61 is an analogous estimate based on boiled rice noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in approved sources. 5. is_halal flag set to false as halal certification status for this specific dish preparation is not confirmed in the HPB source; consumers should verify with the specific vendor. 6. Serving size of 400 g is an estimate for a standard hawker-centre bowl; actual serving sizes may vary between 350–500 g.
